title: Simulation-based development: shaping clinical procedures for extra-uterine life support technology
author: van Haren, J. S.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Background:  Research into Artificial Placenta and Artificial Womb (APAW) technology for extremely premature infants (born < 28 weeks of gestation) is currently being conducted in animal studies and shows promising results. Because of the unprecedented nature of this potential treatment and the high risk and low incidence of occurrence, translation to human applications is complex. Consequently, the obstetric procedure of transferring the infant from the pregnant woman to the APAW system has not yet been established for human patients. Simulation-based, user-centered development provides a safe environment where protocols and devices can be conceptualized and tested. Our aim is to use participatory design principles in a simulation context to gain and integrate user perspectives in the early design phase of a protocol for this novel procedure. 
  Methods:  Simulation protocols and prototypes were developed using an iterative participatory design approach. Usability testing, including general and task-specific feedback, was obtained from participants with clinical expertise from a range of disciplines. The procedure utilized fetal and maternal manikins and included animations and protocol task cards. 
  Results:  Physical simulation with active clinician participation led to the diffusion of tacit knowledge and an iteratively formed shared understanding of the requirements and values needed in the procedure. At each iteration, participant input was translated into simulation protocols and design adjustments. 
  Conclusion:  This work demonstrates that simulation-based participatory design can aid in shaping future clinical procedure and product development and rehearsing future implementation with healthcare professionals.
